Operating in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), and owned by [J. L. Wilkinson](/wiki/J._L._Wilkinson "J. L. Wilkinson was the first white owner at the time of the establishment of the team.[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1) In 1930, the Monarchs became the first professional baseball team to use a portable lighting system which was transported from game to game in trucks to play games at night, five years before any [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team did.[[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) The Monarchs won ten league championships before integration, and triumphed in the first [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series") in 1924. The Monarchs had only one season in which they did not have a winning record[[3]](#cite_note-K-State-3) and produced more major league players than any other Negro league franchise.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After relocating to Grand Rapids, Michigan, the team was disbanded in [1965](/wiki/1965_in_baseball "1965 in baseball").

The **Kansas City Monarchs** were the longest-running franchise in the history of [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball")'s [Negro leagues](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball"). Operating in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), and owned by [J. L. Wilkinson](/wiki/J._L._Wilkinson "J. L. Wilkinson"), they were charter members of the [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1920%E2%80%9331) "Negro National League (1920–31)") from [1920](/wiki/1920_in_sports "1920 in sports") to [1930](/wiki/1930_in_sports "1930 in sports"). Wilkinson was the first white owner at the time of the establishment of the team.[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1) In 1930, the Monarchs became the first professional baseball team to use a portable lighting system which was transported from game to game in trucks to play games at night, five years before any [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team did.[[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) The Monarchs won ten league championships before integration, and triumphed in the first [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series") in 1924. The Monarchs had only one season in which they did not have a winning record[[3]](#cite_note-K-State-3) and produced more major league players than any other Negro league franchise.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After relocating to Grand Rapids, Michigan, the team was disbanded in [1965](/wiki/1965_in_baseball "1965 in baseball").

## Negro National League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Negro National League")]

For a list of annual win-loss records, see [List of Kansas City Monarchs seasons](/wiki/List_of_Kansas_City_Monarchs_seasons "List of Kansas City Monarchs seasons").

The Monarchs were formed in 1920, primarily from two sources. Owner [J. L. Wilkinson](/wiki/J._L._Wilkinson "J. L. Wilkinson") drew players from his [All Nations](/wiki/All_Nations "All Nations") barnstorming team, which had been inactive during World War I, and the [25th Infantry](/wiki/25th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "25th Infantry Regiment (United States)") Wreckers, an all-black team recruited into the U.S. Army almost exclusively for their playing talent. He put together a collection of talent, including pitcher/outfielder [Bullet Rogan](/wiki/Bullet_Rogan "Bullet Rogan"), an eventual Hall of Famer who established himself as one of the most popular stars of the new league; sluggers [Dobie Moore](/wiki/Dobie_Moore "Dobie Moore"), [Heavy Johnson](/wiki/Heavy_Johnson "Heavy Johnson"), [George Carr](/wiki/George_Carr_(baseball) "George Carr (baseball)"), and [Hurley McNair](/wiki/Hurley_McNair "Hurley McNair"); and pitchers [Rube Currie](/wiki/George_Reuben_%22Rube%22_Currie "George Reuben \"Rube\" Currie") and Cliff Bell. Immediate contenders, the Monarchs became bitter rivals to black baseball's reigning power, [Rube Foster](/wiki/Rube_Foster "Rube Foster")'s [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants"). After three years of failing to break off the Giants' hold on the pennant, Wilkinson fired manager [Sam Crawford](/wiki/Sam_Crawford_(pitcher) "Sam Crawford (pitcher)") in mid-1923, replacing him with veteran Cuban star [José Méndez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_M%C3%A9ndez "José Méndez"), who then led the Monarchs to the league championship.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4a/Frank_Duncan_1924.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Duncan_1924.jpg)

Frank Duncan, 1924 Monarchs

Winning the pennant again in [1924](/wiki/1924_in_sports "1924 in sports"), the Monarchs participated in the first [Negro League World Series](/wiki/Negro_League_World_Series "Negro League World Series"), defeating the [Eastern Colored League](/wiki/Eastern_Colored_League "Eastern Colored League") champion [Hilldale](/wiki/Hilldale_Daisies "Hilldale Daisies") team from Darby, Pennsylvania, in a ten-game series (five wins, four losses, and one tie). In this series, Méndez had an [ERA](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average") of 1.42 in four of the games and was responsible for a shutout in the one game he was the starting pitcher in.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Motivated by the Monarchs' runaway pennant victory, NNL president [Rube Foster](/wiki/Rube_Foster "Rube Foster") changed the league schedule to a [split-season](/wiki/Split_season#Negro_leagues "Split season") format for 1925. However, Kansas City still took the league title again in 1925, but lost the World Series to Hilldale when Rogan was injured just before the series began, winning one game and losing five.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
Even though Méndez was the manager, he still pitched during the few years he held the position.[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1) Among the team's regulars during these years were the second baseman/shortstop [Newt Allen](/wiki/Newt_Allen "Newt Allen") who in the 1924 series alone had a batting average of .282 and seven doubles [[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) and [Frank Duncan](/wiki/Frank_Duncan_(baseball,_born_1901) "Frank Duncan (baseball, born 1901)"). [Newt Joseph](/wiki/Newt_Joseph "Newt Joseph") played third base for the Monarchs from 1922 through their NNL years, hitting a composite .284 during that time.[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1)

In 1926 manager Méndez returned to Cuba, and Rogan took over as player/manager. He kept up the Monarchs' tradition of fine pitching, as the team's staff over the next few years featured Negro league greats including [Chet Brewer](/wiki/Chet_Brewer "Chet Brewer"), [William Bell](/wiki/William_Bell_(baseball) "William Bell (baseball)"), and lefty [Andy Cooper](/wiki/Andy_Cooper "Andy Cooper"). The club traded for legendary Cuban outfielder [Cristóbal Torriente](/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Torriente "Cristóbal Torriente"), but also permanently lost the services of star shortstop [Dobie Moore](/wiki/Dobie_Moore "Dobie Moore"), whose career ended that year due to a severe off-the-field injury. After winning the first-half pennant, the Monarchs dropped a best of nine playoff to the [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants") when Rogan lost both games of a series-closing doubleheader to the young [Bill Foster](/wiki/Bill_Foster_(baseball) "Bill Foster (baseball)"), another eventual Hall of Famer. In 1928 the Monarchs narrowly missed a second-half title, but won both halves of the 1929 NNL title with the best overall single-season record ever compiled by a Negro league team at 62 wins and 17 losses. By this time, pitcher [Andy Cooper](/wiki/Andy_Cooper "Andy Cooper") who had made a name for himself by playing for seven years with the Detroit Stars had joined the Monarchs.[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1) No World Series was played that year between the Monarchs and the [Baltimore Black Sox](/wiki/Baltimore_Black_Sox "Baltimore Black Sox"), champions of the eastern [American Negro League](/wiki/American_Negro_League "American Negro League").

## J.L. Wilkinson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: J.L. Wilkinson")]

J.L. Wilkinson was the owner of the Kansas City Monarchs team from 1920 until the team was sold to Tom Baird in 1947. J.L. Wilkinson was the first person to use days like "[Ladies' Day](/wiki/Ladies%27_Day_(baseball) "Ladies' Day (baseball)")" and "Kid's Day". These days were used to promote baseball and attract more players to the All Nationals Club to develop their talent and eventually be available to be placed on the Kansas City Monarch team. He was among the top businessmen and promoters for African American baseball. This helped the Kansas City Monarchs become a very stable and successful franchise. J.L. Wilkinson's Kansas City Monarchs was the only team in the Negro Leagues to play against a comedic team even after there were regulations put in place so that other teams could avoid playing against the comedic team. This comedic team wore grass skirts, war paint, and bright uniforms with a clown on the front.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Night baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Night baseball")]

The Monarchs won four pennant championships before they introduced night baseball in the 1930s. The Kansas City Monarchs started playing night baseball to try to get more people to come to the games. the first night game was in early march, 1930 in Lawrence, Kansas. The Monarchs had portable light systems that could be transported on the team's bus to any game. The lights were powered by portable generators and attached to retractable poles. This was the first team to regularly play baseball under artificial light, including the major league teams. Night baseball gave the Monarchs more time to play more games, which also allowed them to make more profits. This increase in profits helped the Kansas City Monarchs continue to be one of the most stable franchises in the Negro Leagues.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Barnstorming, Negro American League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Barnstorming, Negro American League")]

Following the death of the original league, the Monarchs spent several years as an independent team, mostly [barnstorming](/wiki/Barnstorm_(sports) "Barnstorm (sports)") through the Midwest, West, and western Canada. They frequently toured with the [House of David](/wiki/House_of_David_(commune) "House of David (commune)") baseball team. Hall of Famers [Hilton Smith](/wiki/Hilton_Smith "Hilton Smith"), a pitcher, and [Willard Brown](/wiki/Willard_Brown_(outfielder) "Willard Brown (outfielder)"), a slugging shortstop/outfielder with a consistent batting average of over .300,[[1]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006b-1) became Monarch mainstays during this time. During the 1940s, Willard Brown became the go-to home run hitter for the Monarchs.[[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) With [Andy Cooper](/wiki/Andy_Cooper "Andy Cooper") now at the helm, the Monarchs became charter members of the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League") (NAL) in 1937, winning the first league title. Andy Cooper was responsible for leading the Monarchs to bring home the pennant in 1939 and 1940.[[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) The Kansas City Monarchs then won the next two league championships and won [[3]](#cite_note-K-State-3) winning the renewed [Negro League World Series](/wiki/1942_Negro_World_Series "1942 Negro World Series") in [1942](/wiki/1942_in_sports "1942 in sports") in four straight games against the [Homestead Grays](/wiki/Homestead_Grays "Homestead Grays").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg/250px-Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg)

[Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson")

At the start of this run the Monarchs acquired their most famous player, Hall of Fame pitcher [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige"), who had since his rookie season in 1927 built a reputation as the best hurler in black baseball for the [Birmingham Black Barons](/wiki/Birmingham_Black_Barons "Birmingham Black Barons"), [Pittsburgh Crawfords](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Crawfords "Pittsburgh Crawfords"), and several other teams.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Suffering from an arm injury and generally thought to be done, Paige joined the Monarchs' B team in 1939; by 1940 he had recovered and been called up to the Monarchs' main squad, where he became their top drawing card. Paige was the subject of a lot of stories, both true and folklore, and became a legend to people who don't even follow baseball. For example, he was known to have known the outfielders to sit on the ground behind him while he struck out the hitter and there was someone on base that could possibly tie the game up. Paige also warmed up before pitching in a game by throwing across a gum wrapper as home plate.[[2]](#cite_note-Goldstein-2006a-2) Paige led another superb Monarchs' staff that included fellow Hall of Famer [Hilton Smith](/wiki/Hilton_Smith "Hilton Smith"), the veteran [Chet Brewer](/wiki/Chet_Brewer "Chet Brewer"), [Booker McDaniels](/wiki/Booker_McDaniels "Booker McDaniels"), [Jim LaMarque](/wiki/Jim_LaMarque "Jim LaMarque"), and several others. They won one last NAL pennant in 1946, but lost a seven-game World Series to the [Newark Eagles](/wiki/Newark_Eagles "Newark Eagles"); in this series, they lost four games and won three.[[10]](#cite_note-exhibits-10)

In 1945, UCLA football star and Army lieutenant [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") hit .387 as the Monarchs' shortstop. He became the first Monarch to make the jump to white baseball, signing with the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/History_of_the_Brooklyn_Dodgers "History of the Brooklyn Dodgers") in 1946. He broke the minor league color line in 1946 with the [Montreal Royals](/wiki/Montreal_Royals "Montreal Royals"), and integrated the major leagues with the Dodgers in 1947. As baseball gradually desegregated in the late 1940s and 1950s, the Monarchs developed a niche as the foremost developer of black talent for the major leagues. The team sent more players to the majors than any other Negro league franchise, including Robinson, Paige, [Ernie Banks](/wiki/Ernie_Banks "Ernie Banks"), [Elston Howard](/wiki/Elston_Howard "Elston Howard"), [Hank Thompson](/wiki/Hank_Thompson_(baseball) "Hank Thompson (baseball)"), and [Willard Brown](/wiki/Willard_Brown "Willard Brown").

[Newt Allen](/wiki/Newt_Allen "Newt Allen") succeeded Cooper as manager in 1941, and was followed by [Frank Duncan](/wiki/Frank_Duncan_(baseball,_born_1901) "Frank Duncan (baseball, born 1901)") in 1942. Duncan stayed at the helm through the 1947 season winning two league titles and one world title. After Duncan stepped down, longtime first baseman [Buck O'Neil](/wiki/Buck_O%27Neil "Buck O'Neil") took over. Then Monarchs lost the league title to the Birmingham Black Barons in 1948 which prevented them from appearing in the last Negro World Series. 1948 was also the year that Wilkinson sold the Monarchs to [Tom Baird](/wiki/Tom_Baird "Tom Baird") who owned the team through their minor league days in the 1950s.[[3]](#cite_note-K-State-3) The Monarchs won the league's western division first-half pennant in 1949, but declined to participate in a playoff with the [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants"), as their roster was depleted by player sales to major league clubs. They won the NAL West Division title in 1950 but did not meet the eastern champion [Indianapolis Clowns](/wiki/Indianapolis_Clowns "Indianapolis Clowns") that year. They won a half-season pennant in 1951 but lost a playoff. O'Neil won his only two league titles in 1953 and 1955, with a last-place finish sandwiched between in 1954 as the Negro American League of the 1950s declined in quality and shrank in size, while in the process grooming a number of eventual major league players.

## Home fields and move to Grand Rapids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Home fields and move to Grand Rapids")]

The Monarchs played their home games in the minor league [Kansas City Blues](/wiki/Kansas_City_Blues_(American_Association) "Kansas City Blues (American Association)")' Association Park from 1920 to 1923, and moved to the Blues' new park, Muehlebach Field, in mid-1923. They mostly barnstormed in the early-to-mid-1930s, but used Muehlebach (later known as Ruppert Stadium or Blues Stadium at different times) from 1937 until [1955](/wiki/1955_in_baseball "1955 in baseball"). They once played a game against the Brownsville Merchants at [Charro Park](/wiki/Charro_Park "Charro Park") in 1946.[[11]](#cite_note-11) After a single season of scheduling games with the major league [Kansas City Athletics](/wiki/Kansas_City_Athletics "Kansas City Athletics") as prime tenants of the renamed Municipal Stadium, Tom Baird sold eight players to major league clubs and four more players to minor league teams,[[12]](#cite_note-12) released his manager, [Buck O'Neil](/wiki/Buck_O%27Neil "Buck O'Neil"), who then signed on as a scout for the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs"),[[10]](#cite_note-exhibits-10) and then sold the franchise to baseball entrepreneur Ted Rasberry, who moved its base to [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"), though retaining the name "Kansas City Monarchs".[[13]](#cite_note-Kansas_City_Star-13) From 1956 on, the Monarchs were a full-time barnstorming team. The Negro American League ceased operations in [1962](/wiki/1962_in_baseball "1962 in baseball"), and the Monarchs finally disbanded after the [1965](/wiki/1965_in_baseball "1965 in baseball") season. In Grand Rapids, the Monarchs Played at Valley Field, located at 700 Valley Ave NW, near where Sullivan Field is today.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Minor league affiliate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Minor league affiliate")]

The Kansas City Monarchs were one of a few Negro league teams that informally employed a farm team. The [Monroe Monarchs](/wiki/Monroe_Monarchs "Monroe Monarchs") played from the late 1920s to 1935, mostly as a minor league team loosely associated with Kansas City.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Legacy")]

In February 2021, the team's name was revived by a Kansas City, Kansas, minor league team, the [Kansas City T-Bones](/wiki/Kansas_City_T-Bones "Kansas City T-Bones"). The name was approved through a negotiation with the Negro Leagues Baseball Museum.[[16]](#cite_note-KMBZ-16)

### MLB throwback jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kansas_City_Monarchs&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: MLB throwback jerseys")]

The [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") have honored the Monarchs by wearing replica uniforms during regular-season baseball games on several occasions, including July 14, 2001 (at Pittsburgh), July 1, 2007, and May 30, 2009 (at home vs. White Sox), June 9, 2012 (at Pittsburgh), July 21, 2012, and June 23, 2019 (both at home vs. Minnesota), August 24, 2013 (at home vs. Washington Nationals), May 18, 2014 (at home vs. Baltimore), May 17, 2015 (at home vs. New York Yankees), May 15, 2016 (at home vs. Atlanta), May 7, 2017 (at home vs. Cleveland), and August 13, 2022 (at home vs. Los Angeles Dodgers). Throwback jerseys worn during Royals home games have typically been auctioned as a fundraiser for the [Negro Leagues Baseball Museum](/wiki/Negro_Leagues_Baseball_Museum "Negro Leagues Baseball Museum").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
